title: Evaluate PrismML Bonsai-8B 1-bit as mogfish inference model
description:
PrismML released Bonsai-8B (Apache 2.0, 2026-03-31): 8B parameter model at 1.15GB, claims competitive with Qwen3-8B on IFEval/GSM8K/MMLU-Redux. 131 tok/s on M4 Pro, 40 tok/s on iPhone 17 Pro. Ships in GGUF and MLX formats on HuggingFace (prism-ml/Bonsai-8B-mlx-1bit, prism-ml/Bonsai-8B-gguf).

Relevance to mogfish: 8B model at 1.15GB fits the <1GB-RAM VM deployment target (barely) with dramatically better capacity than our current 1B Gemma 3. If annotation/classification/mog-generation quality is good enough, this replaces the 1B model entirely.

LoRA fine-tuning on 1-bit models is demonstrated: QVAC Fabric (Tether) runs BitNet LoRA on consumer GPUs and phones. BitLoRA paper published in Expert Systems with Applications. mlx-lm likely supports LoRA on the MLX variant.

Caution: no independent benchmarks exist yet (launched yesterday). PrismML's 'intelligence density' metric is proprietary marketing. Quality claims are unverified.

Next steps: (1) download Bonsai-8B GGUF/MLX, (2) run mogfish acceptance tests against base model, (3) if promising, LoRA fine-tune on our 3,509 expanded annotation examples, (4) compare quality and latency against fused Gemma 1B.
context: docs/lab-notebook.md, mogfish.md Deployment Constraints, prism-ml/Bonsai-8B-mlx-1bit on HuggingFace, QVAC Fabric BitNet LoRA framework

note:
Renamed to inv-model-architecture-pivot. Broadened scope to cover both Bonsai-8B 1-bit and the MoE expert pruning approach from docs/plans/moe-pivot.md. Both are paths to 8B-class quality at <1.5GB for phone/VM deployment.

Two competing approaches:

1. MoE expert pruning (moe-pivot.md): Start from a pre-trained MoE checkpoint (DeepSeek-V3-Lite ~16B total / ~2.4B active is the best fit), LoRA fine-tune on mogfish data, profile expert activation, prune unused experts, quantize. Results in a domain-specialized MoE that's smaller than the original but retains 8B-class routing capacity. Requires flash-moe SSD streaming for phone deployment. More complex pipeline but well-understood theory.

2. 1-bit native (Bonsai-8B): PrismML's 8B at 1.15GB, claims competitive with Qwen3-8B. LoRA on 1-bit models demonstrated by QVAC Fabric and BitLoRA paper. Ships GGUF+MLX. Simpler deployment (no SSD streaming, no expert management) but unverified quality — no independent benchmarks, launched 2026-03-31.

Evaluation plan: (1) Run mogfish acceptance tests against base Bonsai-8B. (2) If quality is close to our fused Gemma 1B, LoRA fine-tune Bonsai on expanded 3,509 examples. (3) Compare annotation/classify/generate_mog quality and latency against Gemma 1B and against a pruned MoE if we pursue that path. (4) The deployment constraint is the tiebreaker — whichever fits in <1GB RAM on a CPU-only VM with acceptable latency wins.

The MoE path has higher ceiling (more parameters available for routing) but more engineering complexity. The 1-bit path is simpler but the quality floor is unknown. Both should be evaluated before committing.

title: mmap'd model weights shared across multiple mogfish instances
description: mistral.rs already uses mmap for model loading. When multiple mogfish instances run on the same machine, the OS shares the same physical pages for the mmap'd UQFF and residual files. First instance pages in ~1.2GB, subsequent instances add only KV cache + runtime overhead (~50-100MB each). This is free — no code changes needed, just a deployment property to document and verify. Relevant when mogfish runs as a per-user daemon or when multiple shells share the annotator.

title: generate_mog acceptance test passes on annotation JSON, not Mog scripts
description: The generate_mog_returns_nonempty_script test only asserts !script.is_empty(). The expanded-v1 model returns annotation JSON (not a Mog script) and the test passes. The model never learned Mog generation: the expanded training dataset oversampled annotation 3x, drowning out 1,754 Mog generation examples. System prompt was also wrong. Test fixed to validate Mog syntax (requires/fn main). Model needs retraining with balanced data.
